target.title: docs: remove turnkey hosting service docs
target.body:
Related: #94584

## What Problem This Solves

Resolves a docs placement problem where the public hosting docs promoted turnkey third-party OpenClaw hosting paths after maintainers decided core docs should not endorse third-party hosting services.

## Why This Change Was Made

The hosting docs now focus on infrastructure users administer themselves. Hostinger is kept as a self-managed VPS guide, the hosted-template pages for Railway, Northflank, Render, and Upstash Box are removed from the docs source, and their old routes now redirect to the generic Linux server guide.

This PR was prepared with Codex.

## User Impact

Users can still find VPS, VM, container, Kubernetes, and local server hosting guidance, including a Hostinger VPS path. They will no longer be routed from OpenClaw docs to turnkey third-party service pages.

## Evidence

- `pnpm docs:list`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 docs\vps.md docs\install\hostinger.md docs\install\index.md`
- `pnpm docs:check-mdx`
- `pnpm docs:check-links`
- `pnpm docs:check-i18n-glossary`
- `git diff --check`
- `node -e "JSON.parse(require('fs').readFileSync('docs/docs.json','utf8')); console.log('docs.json ok')"`

target.title: fix: macOS-only Parallels smoke harness regression
target.body:
## Summary

Found one macOS-only Parallels smoke harness regression. The new guest script writer creates a root-owned `700` script, then the macOS lane executes it as the desktop user.

## What ClawSweeper Is Fixing

- **Low: macOS update script is not readable by the user that executes it** (reliability)
  - File: `scripts/e2e/parallels/npm-update-smoke.ts:739`
  - Evidence: `guestMacos()` writes the script via `writeGuestScript(macosVm, ...)`, then runs it through `prlctl exec ... --current-user ... /bin/bash <scriptPath>` or the sudo desktop-user fallback at lines 451-453. But `writeGuestScript()` writes with plain `prlctl exec <vm> /usr/bin/tee <scriptPath>` and then `chmod 700 <scriptPath>` at lines 730-739. The Parallels docs describe `--current-user` as logging in as the current guest OS user, so the write path and execute path are different identities for the macOS lane. ([docs.parallels.com](https://docs.parallels.com/landing/parallels-desktop-developers-guide/command-line-interface-utility/manage-virtual-machines-from-cli/general-virtual-machine-management/execute-a-command-in-a-virtual-machine))
  - Impact: `pnpm test:parallels:npm-update -- --platform macos` can fail before it exercises `openclaw update`, likely with `/bin/bash: /tmp/openclaw-parallels-npm-update-macos-...sh: Permission denied`. That breaks the intended release/update smoke coverage on current `main`; no later commit in the touched files fixes it.
  - Suggested fix: for macOS, write/chmod the temp script through the same resolved transport used to execute it, or explicitly `chown` it to the resolved desktop user and keep permissions non-world-readable because the script embeds provider credentials. Keep the Linux root-owned path separate.
  - Confidence: high

## Expected Repair Surface

- `scripts/e2e/parallels/npm-update-smoke.ts`
- `scripts/e2e/parallels/npm-update-scripts.ts`

## Source And Review Context

- ClawSweeper report: https://github.com/openclaw/clawsweeper/blob/main/records/openclaw-openclaw/commits/af5a1fbddb14a1f1ef2f6e9f3487f108d54ce066.md
- Commit under review: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/af5a1fbddb14a1f1ef2f6e9f3487f108d54ce066
- Latest main at intake: e311ffdcb94e760796a0a8a2c7e58fb8223678bf
- Original commit author: Peter Steinberger
- GitHub author: @steipete
- Highest severity: low
- Review confidence: high

- Diff: `231e5c618f8ae700d51d080e4182a57588da888b..af5a1fbddb14a1f1ef2f6e9f3487f108d54ce066`
- Changed files: `scripts/e2e/parallels/npm-update-scripts.ts`, `scripts/e2e/parallels/npm-update-smoke.ts`
- Code read: full changed files, `scripts/AGENTS.md`, Parallels smoke skill, adjacent guest transport helpers, macOS/Linux smoke helpers, package script entrypoint
- Dependencies/web: official Parallels `prlctl exec` docs; no package dependency changes
- Commands: `pnpm docs:list`, `git show`, `git diff`, `git diff --check`, `pnpm install`, `pnpm exec tsx scripts/e2e/parallels/npm-update-smoke.ts --help`, `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 ...`

## Expected validation

- `pnpm check:changed`

ClawSweeper already ran:
- `pnpm exec tsx scripts/e2e/parallels/npm-update-smoke.ts --help`: passed after installing deps
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 scripts/e2e/parallels/npm-update-scripts.ts scripts/e2e/parallels/npm-update-smoke.ts`: passed
- `git diff --check ...`: passed

Known review limits:
- `prlctl` is not installed in this environment, so I could not run the actual VM smoke.

## ClawSweeper Guardrails

- Re-check the finding against latest `main` before changing code.
- Keep the patch to the narrowest behavior change and matching regression coverage.
- Do not merge automatically; this PR stays for maintainer review.

target.title: perf(imessage): show typing sooner for slow replies
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Live iMessage turns can feel sluggish when the accepted message enters expensive setup before the model starts responding. In the profiled path, iMessage dispatch itself was quick, but context/session/model setup could leave the sender with no immediate feedback.

Read receipts were also on the accepted-turn critical path, so a slow private API `read` RPC could delay dispatch before the agent even started.

## Why This Change Was Made

This moves iMessage private-API UI feedback off the critical path:

- accepted direct iMessage turns now send an early native typing cue before attachment/history/context/model setup;
- early typing is gated to direct, allowed sessions that use the instant/default typing mode and have `imsg` typing support;
- early typing is explicitly stopped after dispatch completion or pre-dispatch failure, including fast command paths that never activate the normal typing controller;
- read receipts are now best-effort and use a short-lived RPC client, so a slow `read` call cannot block dispatch or the monitor client's watch stream.

The direct typing target reuses the existing iMessage reply target builder, so SMS-vs-iMessage service selection remains consistent with final replies.

## User Impact

Users most likely to benefit are people using OpenClaw over iMessage direct messages where responses can take several seconds because of long context, tool setup, model latency, or context-engine work. They should see the iMessage typing bubble sooner after the turn is accepted, which improves perceived responsiveness even when the underlying model path is still slow.

Required setup for the visible typing/read-receipt benefit:

- iMessage channel enabled and receiving accepted direct-message turns;
- `imsg` private API bridge available via `imsg launch`;
- `imsg status --json` reporting RPC methods that include `typing` for early typing, and `read` for read receipts;
- `session.typingMode` / `agents.defaults.typingMode` unset or set to `instant`;
- source delivery not denied by session send policy;
- direct chats only for the early typing cue. Group chats keep the existing behavior so group traffic does not show broad typing feedback.

The read-receipt optimization benefits hosts with private-API `read` support when `channels.imessage.sendReadReceipts` is not disabled.

## Performance Savings

Live dogfood runs showed the user-visible read/typing feedback delay drop from about `10s` send-to-read before this work to about `2s` after moving the read receipt off the accepted-turn critical path. On the same setup, disabling the older 7s iMessage split-send debounce produced about `1s` send-to-read, which confirmed this PR removes the private-API read RPC from the hot path and leaves the remaining delay to debounce/model/context work.

For slow model/context turns, the savings are mostly perceived responsiveness: the native iMessage typing cue is emitted before attachment/history/context/model setup, so users get immediate channel feedback while the expensive turn continues.

## Evidence

Fresh live iMessage proof on commit `26a1d38904` (`2026-06-23`):

- Ran Gateway from the PR checkout in the foreground after stopping the installed LaunchAgent; startup printed `OpenClaw 2026.6.9 (26a1d38)` and loaded `extensions/imessage` from this checkout.
- A real direct iMessage inbound row arrived from a separate device/account: DB row `6340`, `is_from_me=0`, text `Test`, created `2026-06-23T14:20:55.574Z`.
- The PR checkout processed that row after startup catch-up and marked it read: DB row `6340` had `is_read=1` and non-zero `date_read=803917326885792000`.
- No `imessage: mark read failed` or typing failure log appeared during the validation window.

Redacted live log sequence from the same PR checkout run:

```text
2026-06-23T07:22:06.402-07:00 [routing] resolveAgentRoute: channel=imessage accountId=default peer=direct:*** bindings=0
2026-06-23T07:22:06.416-07:00 imessage inbound: chatId=3 from=imessage:*** len=276 preview="[iMessage *** +8h ...] ***: Test ..."
2026-06-23T07:22:07.026-07:00 message received: channel=imessage chatId=imessage:*** messageId=896 source=dispatchInboundMessage
2026-06-23T07:22:10.645-07:00 message queued: sessionKey=agent:lobster:imessage:direct:*** source=dispatch queueDepth=1 sessionState=idle
2026-06-23T07:22:10.674-07:00 message dispatch started: channel=imessage sessionKey=agent:lobster:imessage:direct:*** source=replyResolver
2026-06-23T07:22:22.449-07:00 preflightCompaction skipped: sessionKey=agent:lobster:imessage:direct:*** runtime=codex reason=codex_native_auto_compaction
2026-06-23T07:22:22.482-07:00 memoryFlush check: sessionKey=agent:lobster:imessage:direct:*** transcriptBytes=2509900 forceFlushByTranscriptSize=true
2026-06-23T07:22:49.109-07:00 message dispatch completed: channel=imessage sessionKey=agent:lobster:imessage:direct:*** source=replyResolver outcome=completed duration=38435ms
2026-06-23T07:22:49.111-07:00 message processed: channel=imessage chatId=imessage:*** messageId=896 outcome=completed duration=42081ms
```

Older local live proof:

- Live dogfood read-status proof on the same host/setup: before this change, send-to-read feedback was roughly `10s`; with this PR it was roughly `2s`; disabling the older 7s inbound debounce on the same setup reached roughly `1s`. That shows this PR removes the private-API read RPC from the accepted-turn hot path and leaves remaining delay to debounce/model/context work.
- `imsg status` confirmed advanced IMCore features were available, including read receipts and typing.
- Explicit private-API timing checks during validation: `imsg read --chat-id 7 --json` returned in `0.06s`, `0.07s`, and `0.11s`; `imsg typing --chat-id 7 --duration 1s --json` returned in `1.15s`, `1.20s`, and `1.21s`.

Local proof:

- `git diff --check` passed.
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/imessage/src/monitor.last-route.test.ts` passed, 33 tests.
- `.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode branch --base origin/main` reported no accepted/actionable findings.
- `pnpm exec oxlint extensions/imessage/src/monitor/monitor-provider.ts` passed after the lint follow-up.

Current PR CI proof on commit `26a1d389044`:

- [`Real behavior proof`](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/actions/runs/28004825451/job/82884478716) passed.
- [`check-lint`](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/actions/runs/28003203017/job/82879712864) passed after the lint follow-up.
- [`check-additional-extension-bundled`](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/actions/runs/28003203017/job/82879712863) passed after the lint follow-up.
- Full PR check set has no pending or failing checks after the proof-label cleanup.

Docs updated: https://docs.openclaw.ai/channels/imessage

target.title: fix(gateway): cap auth limiter entries
target.body:
## Summary

Caps the Gateway auth rate limiter's tracked client entries so failed-auth bookkeeping cannot grow without bound between prune sweeps.

## Changes

- Adds a normalized internal `maxEntries` option with a default cap of 10,000 tracked identities.
- Prunes expired entries before eviction, evicts the oldest unlocked entry when at capacity, and preserves active lockouts.
- Fails closed with a temporary overflow lock when every tracked entry is actively locked, avoiding a fail-open saturated-table path.
- Adds focused Gateway auth limiter tests for flood capping, lockout-preserving eviction, saturated overflow behavior, and numeric normalization.

## Validation

- `corepack pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/gateway/auth-rate-limit.ts src/gateway/auth-rate-limit.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/auth-rate-limit.test.ts` (4 files, 136 tests)
- `.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode local` (clean)

## Notes

- Closes openclaw/openclaw#77986.
- AI-assisted.
- `CHANGELOG.md` was not updated.

target.title: Clear pending tool media delivered via the messaging tool
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Fixes an issue where users receive a generated image **twice** in chat. In automatic visible-reply mode, a media-producing tool (e.g. `image_generate`) queues its output for automatic source-reply delivery, and when the model *also* sends that same file explicitly via the `message` tool, both deliveries fire. Observed in production as two `sendPhoto` calls for a single `image_generate` completion run.

## Why This Change Was Made

The existing reply-payload dedup (`filterMessagingToolMediaDuplicates`) already filters reply media against `messagingToolSentMediaUrls`, but it compares **normalized full paths** (`normalizeMediaForDedupe` only strips `file://`). The same generated file is referenced by two different prefixes that never normalize to the same string:

- the pending queue holds the **host** path: `/data/media/tool-image-generation/gen-….jpg`
- the `message`-tool args carry the **sandbox-container** path: `/workspace/media/tool-image-generation/gen-….jpg`

So the duplicate slips past the full-path comparison. This change stops it at the source: when messaging-tool media is committed in `handleToolExecutionEnd`, it drops any `pendingToolMediaUrls` entry with the same **basename** (basename matching is what bridges the host/container prefix difference). The drop is gated on a non-error send, so a failed `message`-tool send keeps the pending media as the fallback delivery path. Non-goal: it does not change `filterMessagingToolMediaDuplicates` or other delivery paths.

`pendingToolAudioAsVoice` / `pendingToolTrustedLocalMedia` are **global** flags (set per queue, not per URL). When the basename filter empties `pendingToolMediaUrls`, those flags are now cleared too — otherwise `readPendingToolMediaReply` would still return a non-null **metadata-only payload** (its guard requires both an empty URL list *and* both flags false), producing a phantom fallback reply after the `message` tool already delivered the file. (Addresses the review's `[P2]`/`[P1]` correctness finding.)

## User Impact

Users no longer get the same generated image delivered twice when the assistant both auto-attaches tool media and sends it via the `message` tool. No change to single-delivery cases.

## Evidence

Rebased onto latest `main` (clean, no conflicts). `tsgo:core` clean; `pnpm db:kysely:check` / `check:architecture` pass on the rebased branch (the earlier CI architecture failure was pre-existing main drift in `src/infra/sqlite-user-version.ts`, not this diff).

Five regression tests in `embedded-agent-subscribe.handlers.tools.media.test.ts`, all driving the **real** `handleToolExecutionEnd` handler:

- drops queued tool media already delivered via the message tool across host/container paths
- keeps queued tool media the message tool did **not** deliver
- does **not** drop queued media on a failed message-tool send
- **clears audio/trusted flags when the message tool drained the queued media** — asserts `readPendingToolMediaReply` returns `null` afterward (no phantom fallback reply)
- **keeps audio/trusted flags when some queued media remains undelivered** (partial drain)

```
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  33 passed (33)
```

Both new flag tests are load-bearing: with the source fix reverted, `clears audio/trusted flags …` fails (the queue empties but the flags stay `true`, so `readPendingToolMediaReply` returns a non-null metadata-only reply). The original host/container dedup test likewise fails on `main` without the source change.

Root cause confirmed in production: two `sendPhoto` calls logged for one `image_generate` run.

> **Live transport proof:** a real-Telegram Crabbox before/after recording is the one outstanding `[P1]`. The contributor environment for this branch does not have the Crabbox / Convex burner-account tooling provisioned, so it can't be captured here. A maintainer can capture it via Mantis with the suggested comment, or trigger the standard `telegram-crabbox-e2e-proof` flow.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

target.title: docs(gateway): document high-memory local Ollama compaction tuning
target.body:
﻿Fixes #55888

## What Problem This Solves

High-memory local Ollama users have existing compaction knobs that can keep more recent transcript in large-context sessions, but the local models guide did not explain the supported profile, threshold math, or safe verification path. Users had to infer why a 32k context model started memory flush or preflight compaction earlier than expected.

## Why This Change Was Made

This adds a focused docs-only profile to the local models guide for 32 GB / 64 GB+ Ollama setups. It documents the existing `agents.defaults.compaction` keys, explains the `contextWindow - reserveTokensFloor - memoryFlush.softThresholdTokens` threshold calculation, and keeps runtime defaults unchanged for lower-memory machines.

## User Impact

Users running larger local Ollama contexts can now tune compaction intentionally, see the expected 32,768 - 2,000 - 4,000 = 26,768 threshold example, and confirm the effective `contextWindow` and `threshold` in verbose Gateway logs before relying on a long session.

## Evidence

AI-assisted: yes. I used Codex to inspect the issue, source-backed threshold behavior, docs rules, and validation output. I reviewed the final diff and understand the change.

Validation run locally:

```text
git diff --check
# passed

node scripts/docs-list.js
# passed

node scripts/check-docs-mdx.mjs docs README.md
# Docs MDX check passed (684 files, 9324ms).

node scripts/docs-link-audit.mjs
# checked_internal_links=5464, broken_links=0

node scripts/format-docs.mjs --check
# failed on pre-existing docs/reference/templates/CLAUDE.md, not touched by this PR
```

Attempted the requested pnpm docs commands too, but local pnpm ran a dependency status/install path before the scripts and failed on Windows `EPERM` renames inside `node_modules` (`@types/node` / `@lit/context`). The equivalent Node docs scripts above were run directly and passed for the changed docs surface.

target.title: fix(feishu): add diagnostic detail when inbound message event parsing fails
target.body:
## Summary

**Problem**: When the Lark SDK dispatches inbound Feishu WebSocket message events with unexpected shapes, `parseFeishuMessageEventPayload` silently returns null and the caller logs `ignoring malformed message event payload` with no indication of which field was missing or what the actual event structure looked like. Operators cannot diagnose the root cause without redeploying with extra instrumentation.

**Solution**: Add `formatParseFailureDetail()` that inspects the event payload and reports precisely which required fields are absent. The structured detail is appended to the existing error log so operators can trace the root cause from existing log output.

**What changed**: 
- `extensions/feishu/src/monitor.message-handler.ts`: +38/-1 — new `formatParseFailureDetail()` helper + update to error log line

**What did NOT change**:
- Successful event parsing paths — completely untouched
- Event processing behavior — identical, only the error log content changed
- Any other feishu module

Fixes #92595

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

**Behavior addressed**: Inbound Feishu WebSocket events whose parsed shape doesn't match expectations were silently dropped with a generic log. Operators had no way to identify which field caused the parse failure without modifying and redeploying the code.

**Real setup tested**:
- **Runtime**: Node 24.13, Linux x86_64
- **Branch**: `fix/issue-92595` at `42d094a23` (1 commit)
- **Test runner**: `scripts/run-vitest.mjs` (project-standard wrapper)
- **Vitest**: 4.1.8

**Root cause**: `parseFeishuMessageEventPayload` in `monitor.message-handler.ts` validates incoming WebSocket event payloads against the expected Feishu message event shape. It performs a series of structural checks: is the value a record? Does it have `sender` (a record)? Does `sender` have `sender_id` (a record)? Does it have `message` (a record)? Does `message` have `message_id`, `chat_id`, `chat_type`, `message_type`, and `content`? If any check fails, the function returns `null` — but `null` carries no information about WHICH check failed.

The caller in `createFeishuMessageReceiveHandler` handles the `null` by logging a generic string and returning early. The operator sees the log and knows something was dropped, but has no idea what shape the payload actually had. Without the actual keys or the specific missing field, they can't distinguish between "this is a new event type we don't handle yet" and "the Feishu API changed a field name" and "there's a bug in the Lark SDK's event dispatch."

The fix adds `formatParseFailureDetail(value)` that mirrors the parser's structural checks but produces human-readable descriptions instead of a binary pass/fail. The helper:
1. Checks `isRecord(value)` — if not, reports the actual type
2. Checks `isRecord(value.sender)` — if not, reports available top-level keys
3. Checks `isRecord(value.message)` — same pattern
4. Checks `isRecord(sender.sender_id)` — reports sender's available keys
5. Checks individual message fields (`message_id`, `chat_id`, `chat_type`, `message_type`, `content`) — reports all missing fields

This mirrors `parseFeishuMessageEventPayload`'s exact validation order, so the diagnostic always describes the same structural failure the parser hit. The function reuses the existing `readString` and `normalizeFeishuChatType` helpers, ensuring diagnostic logic doesn't drift from parse logic.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run extensions/feishu/src/monitor.message-handler.test.ts extensions/feishu/src/bot.test.ts` at Node 24.13 Linux x86_64

**After-fix evidence**:
```
[test] starting test/vitest/vitest.extension-feishu.config.ts
 RUN  v4.1.8
 Test Files  2 passed (2)
      Tests  89 passed (89)
[test] passed 1 Vitest shard in 45.68s
```

**Observed result after the fix**: When event parsing fails, the gateway log now shows a detailed reason:

| Scenario | Before | After |
|----------|--------|-------|
| Non-object event | `ignoring malformed message event payload` | `... — value is not a record (type=string)` |
| Missing sender | same generic | `... — missing or invalid sender (keys: event_id, message)` |
| Missing message | same generic | `... — missing or invalid message (keys: event_id, sender)` |
| Missing sender_id | same generic | `... — missing or invalid sender.sender_id (sender keys: sender_type)` |
| Missing chat_type | same generic | `... — missing message fields: chat_type=(missing) (chat_id=oc_xxx)` |
| Missing multiple fields | same generic | `... — missing message fields: chat_id, chat_type=(missing) (chat_id=(missing))` |

The diagnostic is a pure logging improvement: the event processing behavior is identical, only the error log content changed. The `formatParseFailureDetail` function is called only in the error path when `parseFeishuMessageEventPayload` returns null, and its return value is only used as a string appended to the existing log message.

**What was not tested**: Live Feishu WebSocket tenant with real malformed inbound messages. This is a pure diagnostic logging improvement — the behavior of successful parsing paths and error paths is unchanged, only the error message content is richer.

**Evidence provenance**: Terminal output from real local checkout of `fix/issue-92595` on Node 24.13.1 / Linux x86_64, 2026-06-23. The PR is a single clean commit (`42d094a23`) touching only one file.

**Diagnostic coverage matrix** — all 5 failure paths, each with a distinct and useful error message:

| Input shape | Missing field | Output suffix |
|-------------|--------------|---------------|
| Not an object (`null`, string, number) | — | `value is not a record (type=...)` |
| Object with no `sender` key | sender | `missing or invalid sender (keys: event_id, message)` |
| Object with no `message` key | message | `missing or invalid message (keys: event_id, sender)` |
| `sender_id` is not a record | sender.sender_id | `missing or invalid sender.sender_id (sender keys: sender_type)` |
| `message.*` fields missing | various | `missing message fields: chat_type=(missing) (chat_id=oc_xxx)` |

The function uses structured escalation: it checks higher-level structure first (record→sender→message→sender_id) then drills into individual message fields. Each level's error message includes the available keys at that level so operators can see what WAS present in the payload, not just what was missing. For field-level failures, the `chat_id` is always included as context (even when itself missing) because operators need it to identify which channel/conversation the malformed event originated from.

## Tests and validation
| Test Type | Result | Details |
|-----------|--------|---------|
| Unit Tests | ✅ 89/89 | monitor.message-handler.test.ts + bot.test.ts |

## Design decisions

**Why a dedicated failure-detail function instead of inline logging?**

The parse function `parseFeishuMessageEventPayload` returns `null` for failure and has no way to communicate the reason (it doesn't throw, doesn't return an error object). Changing its return type would require updating all callers. The diagnostic function inspects the raw payload independently, following the same structural checks as the parser, so it doesn't need the parser to change. This separation also means the diagnostic code can't accidentally affect the parse result.

**Why include `chat_id` in field-level diagnostics even when it's missing?**

The `chat_id` identifies which Feishu channel/conversation the malformed event came from. Even when `chat_id` itself is one of the missing fields, including it in the diagnostic (as `(missing)`) tells the operator "we couldn't even identify the source channel." When `chat_id` IS present but other fields are broken, it gives operators a concrete target for investigation. The trade-off is intentionally asymmetric: the diagnostic is for human operators, not machines, so rich context matters more than minimal output.

**Why structured escalation instead of checking all fields at once?**

Checking higher-level structure first means we don't try to access `message.chat_type` on a payload that doesn't have a `message` object at all. This prevents confusing error messages like "missing message fields: ..." when the real problem is that `sender` itself is a string instead of an object. The escalation order matches the parser's own field access order, so the diagnostic describes the same failure the parser encountered.

## Risk checklist

**What is the highest-risk area of this change?**

The `formatParseFailureDetail` function accesses `value.sender`, `value.message`, and nested fields. If the input is a record with getter properties that have side effects, the diagnostic function could trigger them. However, these are raw JSON-parsed WebSocket message payloads, which never have getters.

**Risk level**: Low risk — the function is only called in the error path when the event has already failed to parse. It does not mutate any state, does not throw (all field accesses are guarded by `isRecord` checks), and its return value is only used in a log string. The successful parse path is completely untouched.

**Mitigation**: The function follows a structured escalation path with `isRecord` guards at each level, ensuring property accesses only happen on confirmed record types and preventing TypeError throws from accessing properties on non-object values. The `Object.keys()` call is safe on any object type. The `readString` and `normalizeFeishuChatType` helpers are reused directly from the existing parse path to ensure the diagnostic never diverges from the parser's own field interpretation.

**Merge risk declaration**:
- `merge-risk: 🚨 automation` — The error log format changed from a fixed string to a variable format with diagnostic detail appended. This could affect log-based monitoring, alerting rules, or log aggregation queries that match on the exact `ignoring malformed message event payload` text. The new format preserves the original message as a prefix and appends ` — ` followed by diagnostic detail, so substring or prefix-based alerting that matches on `ignoring malformed message event payload` continues to work. Full-string exact-match alerting would need to switch to a prefix or contains match. This is a one-time operational adjustment that affects only the error path, which by definition fires only for malformed events that are already being dropped — no healthy-path behavior is affected.

## Current review state
**What is the next action?** Maintainer review requested. All 89 feishu tests pass with current-head proof.

target.title: docs: require i18n postprocess before skip
target.body:
## Summary
- require docs-i18n postprocess completion before localized outputs become skippable
- mark newly written localized docs as `postprocess_version: pending` and finalize them only after locale-link postprocess succeeds
- add regression coverage for the sticky-failure path where stale English links could otherwise survive retries

## Root cause
- docs-i18n writes translated localized files before `postprocessLocalizedDocs(...)` runs
- those files already carried a fresh `x-i18n.source_hash`, so a later postprocess failure could leave stale English-root links on disk
- on retry with `--overwrite=false`, `shouldSkipDoc(...)` only looked at `source_hash`, so the broken file could be skipped indefinitely unless the English source changed

## Fix
- `encodeFrontMatter(...)` now writes `x-i18n.postprocess_version: pending`
- `postprocessLocalizedDocs(...)` upgrades that marker to the current postprocess version only after it rewrites or finalizes the file
- `shouldSkipDoc(...)` now requires both a matching `source_hash` and the current postprocess version before it skips a localized file

## Test plan
- `go test ./...` in `scripts/docs-i18n`

target.title: perf(qmd): persistent export-state cache + stat fast path in exportSessions
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** For agents with thousands of session transcripts, `QmdMemoryManager.exportSessions()` was the dominant gateway CPU consumer. Every boot and every QMD sync interval walked every session JSONL and called `buildSessionEntry()` per file, which performs full read + parse + redaction + hash/render work even when the transcript export was unchanged.
- **Why it matters:** The previous in-memory skip state only avoided some markdown rewrites after a manager instance had already rebuilt each entry. It did not survive gateway restart, so cold starts and periodic syncs could still create large CPU and GC spikes on production-sized transcript sets.
- **What changed:**
  1. Added a per-agent SQLite cache table, `qmd_session_export_cache`, in `openclaw-agent.sqlite`, keyed by `(session_file, export_dir, render_version)`.
  2. Added a stat + full-content-fingerprint fast path before `buildSessionEntry()`. When cached `size`, `mtimeMs`, `ino`, full content fingerprint, and cached markdown target all match, QMD skips the expensive full-session rebuild.
  3. Preserved the slow path for changed transcripts, missing markdown targets, mismatched cached targets, retention pruning, export-dir changes, and render-version changes.
  4. Kept raw per-agent DB/Kysely access inside the core state owner and exposed only QMD-specific cache helpers through a private local-only SDK subpath, `openclaw/plugin-sdk/memory-core-qmd-export-cache-internal`, scoped to the bundled `memory-core` plugin.
  5. Batch-loaded the current export scope cache rows once per `exportSessions()` pass and reused an in-memory lookup map, so large transcript sets do not reopen/refresh the per-agent DB accessor once per session file.
  6. Excluded the generated private QMD SDK helper artifacts from npm package output: `!dist/plugin-sdk/memory-core-qmd-export-cache-internal.js` and `!dist/plugin-sdk/memory-core-qmd-export-cache-internal.d.ts`.

**Scope boundary:** `renderSessionMarkdown()`, session redaction, line wrapping, and the markdown format QMD consumes are unchanged. `buildSessionEntry()` remains the authoritative slow path. The cache is scoped by export directory and `SESSION_EXPORT_RENDER_VERSION`, so config moves and render-byte changes invalidate cleanly.

**Effect on Eddie's main agent (4,052 sessions / 158 MB) — prior-head context, not current-head proof:**
- Boot QMD sync: **312s -> single-digit seconds**
- Interval QMD sync: **38s -> ~50-500 ms**
- The every-5-min CPU spike + GC tail collapses

> ⚠️ **Benchmark provenance:** these numbers were measured on an early head of
> this branch and were **not** re-run after the SQLite-backed cache rewrite,
> the private-SDK boundary work, the full-content-hash fast path, the
> target-byte fingerprint validation, or the latest `origin/main` merge. They
> remain a faithful description of the *mechanism's* impact (eliminating the
> per-file `buildSessionEntry()` rebuild on unchanged transcripts) but should
> be treated as **observational context, not current-head benchmark proof**.
> Re-running them requires Eddie's live 4,052-session production corpus on the
> running gateway, which is not reproducible from an agent worktree. The
> `## Real Behavior Proof` section below is the authoritative current-head
> behavioral evidence.

## Linked Issue

- Closes openclaw-ys0 (Beads tracking issue)

## Test Coverage

New and updated tests cover the cache contract:

- `extensions/memory-core/src/memory/qmd-manager.test.ts`
  - persists session-export state across manager restarts
  - rebuilds after same-size transcript rewrites, proving content changes are not hidden by matching stat fields
  - ignores cache rows scoped to a previous `exportDir`
  - rebuilds markdown when the cached target has been deleted
  - rebuilds the expected markdown when a current-scope cache row points at another target
  - preserves existing session-export behavior for checkpoint/session filtering
- `src/state/openclaw-agent-db.test.ts`
  - schema shape includes the new derived-state cache table
  - per-agent DB schema metadata and `user_version` remain at version 1
  - newer-schema refusal still protects downgrade safety
- `test/release-check.test.ts`
  - package output rejects private local-only plugin SDK artifacts
  - package `files` exclusions include every private local-only SDK artifact, including the new QMD cache helper

## Tradeoffs / Known Caveats

- The fast path still uses stat identity to avoid hashing obviously changed files, but it verifies a full SHA-1 content fingerprint before skipping the rebuild. Same-size rewrites with preserved or seeded stat identity do not suppress updated markdown.
- `SESSION_EXPORT_RENDER_VERSION` must be bumped when changing `renderSessionMarkdown()`, redaction, line wrapping, or any other logic that changes the bytes written to `qmd/sessions/*.md`.
- The cache table is derived state; the per-agent DB `user_version` remains at 1 for rollback safety.
- No hand-written `CHANGELOG.md` entry is included; current repo policy keeps release notes out of normal PRs and derives them during release generation.

## Real Behavior Proof

- **Behavior addressed:** QMD session export persists its unchanged-transcript fast-path state in the per-agent SQLite DB without exposing raw per-agent DB/Kysely access through the public plugin SDK. It must still rebuild markdown when a cached target is missing, when the cached target points elsewhere, when stat identity matches but the transcript content fingerprint changed, **and when the export target's own bytes were externally modified/corrupted while the source transcript stayed unchanged** (target-byte fingerprint validation; self-heals on the next sync). The private QMD SDK artifact must also stay out of package output.

- **Canonical current-head proof script:** `scripts/proof-77158-qmd-export-target-validation.ts` (run with `pnpm tsx scripts/proof-77158-qmd-export-target-validation.ts`). It drives the real `QmdMemoryManager` against real per-agent SQLite + real fs (no Vitest, no mocked cache seam) across four scenarios: (a) clean cache hit skips rebuild; (b) corrupted target bytes → rebuild + self-heal (the regression the [P2] target-byte finding asked for); (c) source changed → rebuild; (d) legacy NULL `target_fingerprint` row → repopulate when bytes are intact, rebuild when corrupt. Output ends `All runtime assertions passed.` A focused vitest regression (`rebuilds the session-export markdown when the cached target bytes are corrupted`) pins the same path in the unit suite.
- **Real environment tested:** Current PR head `eb71a680cc4339778c93130d923833f98a238b30` in the `perf/qmd-export-stat-fast-path` worktree, after merging current `openclaw/main` and cleaning unrelated formatter-only merge churn, using the real `QmdMemoryManager`, real `resolveMemoryBackendConfig`, the private local-only QMD cache subpath, real per-agent `openclaw-agent.sqlite` storage, and local `qmd 2.1.0` on PATH. The proof ran against an isolated `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR` under `/tmp`; no Vitest harness or mocked QMD export cache was used.
- **Exact steps run after this patch:** `node --import tsx --input-type=module` from the topic worktree with an inline driver. The driver created one session JSONL, ran `QmdMemoryManager.sync({ reason: "manual" })` across fresh manager instances, inspected and seeded `qmd_session_export_cache` through the private QMD helper subpath, rewrote the transcript with a deliberately stale fingerprint row whose size/mtime/inode matched the source, deleted the generated markdown target, and verified the next sync rebuilt it.
- **Evidence after fix:** Captured terminal output from the live run:

```text
current head: eb71a680cc4339778c93130d923833f98a238b30
qmd version: qmd 2.1.0
=== Phase 1: first sync writes markdown and SQLite cache ===
[memory] qmd manager initialized for agent "main" mode=full collections=2 durationMs=899
[memory] qmd sync completed for agent "main" reason=manual durationMs=377
sync 1: sync done in 380 ms
cache target is export file: true
fingerprint length: 40
=== Phase 2: unchanged sync uses fast path ===
[memory] qmd manager initialized for agent "main" mode=full collections=2 durationMs=828
[memory] qmd sync completed for agent "main" reason=manual durationMs=299
sync 2: sync done in 301 ms
export mtime unchanged: true
export size unchanged: true
=== Phase 3: matching stat row with stale content fingerprint rebuilds ===
seeded cache size matches source: true
seeded cache mtime matches source: true
seeded cache ino matches source: true
[memory] qmd manager initialized for agent "main" mode=full collections=2 durationMs=813
[memory] qmd sync completed for agent "main" reason=manual durationMs=306
sync 3: sync done in 308 ms
export mtime newer: true
cache fingerprint changed: true
cache fingerprint length after rebuild: 40
=== Phase 4: delete markdown while SQLite cache survives ===
export exists after delete: false
sqlite cache row still present: true
[memory] qmd manager initialized for agent "main" mode=full collections=2 durationMs=818
[memory] qmd sync completed for agent "main" reason=manual durationMs=307
sync 4: sync done in 309 ms
rebuilt export exists: true
cache target restored to export file: true

All runtime assertions passed.
```

- **Observed result after fix:** The first sync wrote markdown and a SQLite cache row with the expected derived target and 40-character fingerprint. The second sync preserved markdown mtime/size, proving unchanged input took the persisted fast path. The third sync rebuilt after a same-size rewrite even though the seeded cache row matched the source stat fields, proving the full-content fingerprint prevents stale skips. After deleting markdown while leaving SQLite cache intact, the fourth sync regenerated the target and restored the cache target to the expected export file.
- **What was not tested:** I did not rerun a benchmark against Eddie's full 4,052-session production corpus after the SDK-boundary rewrite, base-branch merge, package exclusion fix, cached-target validation fix, target-byte fingerprint validation, and batch-read follow-up. That corpus lives on Eddie's running gateway and is not reproducible from an agent worktree. The proof above (and `scripts/proof-77158-qmd-export-target-validation.ts`) validates the current SQLite cache behavior and all regression paths on real runtime code; the original production-sized timing numbers remain observational context, not current-head benchmark proof (see the ⚠️ caveat under Summary).

## Verification

Current head `33a9ac2566e84594a624ceaacc1520b2ed95a97e` includes:

- `2d3cc42a55` (`perf(qmd): validate cached export target bytes before skip (review feedback)`) — the [P2] target-byte fingerprint validation fix: the fast path now reads the export target and compares its SHA-1 against the cached rendered-markdown fingerprint before skipping; on drift (or a legacy NULL fingerprint) it falls through to the slow path, which self-heals the corrupted target. Adds a nullable `target_fingerprint` column via additive `ALTER` (no schema-version bump).
- `33a9ac2566` (`chore(qmd): refresh plugin SDK API baseline after main merge (review feedback)`) — regenerated `docs/.generated/plugin-sdk-api-baseline.sha256` (tracked file only; `.json`/`.jsonl` are gitignored) after the latest `origin/main` merge shifted the generated SDK surface (corpus refactor #96162 and adjacent memory-host-sdk work). Deterministic across repeated `pnpm plugin-sdk:api:gen` runs.
- `3468c322a6` (`fix(qmd): exclude private cache SDK artifact from package`)
- signed base-refresh merges from current `openclaw/main` to clear the PR's dirty/conflicting merge state
- cleanup restoring unrelated Slack/Telegram/installer/test formatter churn back to current `openclaw/main`, leaving exactly the QMD/cache/SDK/package diff

**Corpus-entry preservation ([P2] #2 in the latest Codex review): verified, no change needed.** Current head's `exportSessions()` already iterates `listSessionTranscriptCorpusEntriesForAgent(this.agentId)` and threads each `corpusEntry` into both `buildSessionEntry()` (`generatedByDreamingNarrative` / `generatedByCronRun`) and `buildSessionArtifactMapping()` — in the fast path AND the slow path — then calls `replaceQmdSessionArtifactMappings(...)`. The `buildSessionArtifactMapping` body is byte-identical to `origin/main`. The accessor-backed corpus identity flow from #96162 is fully preserved; the cache layer short-circuits only the expensive rebuild, never the corpus identity mapping.

**Schema-version-1 derived-cache table — maintainer acceptance item (not a code bug):** the new per-agent `qmd_session_export_cache` table (and its additive `target_fingerprint` column) intentionally keeps `OPENCLAW_AGENT_SCHEMA_VERSION = 1` because it is disposable derived state — a cold/older build simply rebuilds the cache; there is no migration to roll back. This is a deliberate rollback-safety decision that needs a maintainer's explicit acceptance, not a version bump. Flagged for maintainer judgment rather than changed unilaterally.

**Prior art for the version-1 additive-table strategy:** every table ever added to `openclaw-agent.sqlite` has followed the same convention — `CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS` at `user_version = 1`, no version bump, no migration required. The full history on `origin/main`:

| PR / commit | Tables added | Schema version after |
|---|---|---|
| `#88349` (Add per-agent SQLite cache store) | `schema_meta`, `cache_entries` | 1 |
| `#89102` (store auth profiles in sqlite) | `auth_profile_store`, `auth_profile_state` | 1 |
| memory index PRs | `memory_index_meta`, `memory_index_sources`, `memory_index_chunks`, `memory_embedding_cache`, `memory_index_state` | 1 |
| **this PR** | `qmd_session_export_cache` | **1** |

The only time a version bump was attempted (`fix/heartbeat-claude-auth-schema-v2`, a compat shim for v2-stamped agent DBs) it was reverted. The project has consistently treated additive derived-state tables as rollback-safe at version 1; a version bump is reserved for changes that require forward-only migration and must block rollback. `qmd_session_export_cache` contains no user data and can be silently orphaned by a downgrade with no behavioral consequence, which is exactly the class of change this strategy is designed for.

Commands run after the package exclusion fix and/or after the base merge:

```text
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs test/release-check.test.ts
# 56 passed

node scripts/check-plugin-sdk-subpath-exports.mjs
# OK: all referenced openclaw/plugin-sdk/<subpath> imports are exported

node scripts/check-extension-plugin-sdk-boundary.mjs --mode=src-outside-plugin-sdk
# no violations; baseline matches

node scripts/check-extension-plugin-sdk-boundary.mjs --mode=plugin-sdk-internal
# no violations; baseline matches

pnpm plugin-sdk:api:check
# OK docs/.generated/plugin-sdk-api-baseline.sha256

pnpm plugin-sdk:surface:check
# all SDK entrypoints: 339; public package SDK entrypoints: 321; local-only entrypoints: 18; package-exported forbidden subpaths: 0

pnpm exec oxfmt --check package.json docs/.generated/plugin-sdk-api-baseline.sha256 extensions/memory-core/src/memory/qmd-manager.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory/qmd-manager.test.ts src/plugin-sdk/memory-core-qmd-export-cache-internal.ts src/plugins/sdk-alias.ts scripts/lib/plugin-sdk-private-local-only-subpaths.json
# clean

pnpm tsgo:core
# exited 0

pnpm tsgo:core:test
# exited 0

git diff --check
# clean

node --import tsx --input-type=module
# real-runtime QMD/SQLite proof above; All runtime assertions passed.
```

target.title: test(gateway): harden watch regression diagnostics
target.body:
## Summary

This PR hardens the `check-gateway-watch-regression` release/runtime guard so failures are easier to diagnose and short-lived gateway-watch exits are reported accurately.

The change is intentionally narrow. It only touches the gateway watch regression script and its focused tests.

## What Problem This Solves

The watch regression guard currently has a few sharp edges that make release/runtime failures harder to reason about:

- The timed watch command can run under `/bin/sh`, but CI/bootstrap environments may inject shell hooks that assume bash semantics. That can add noisy stderr output or delay gateway readiness before the idle window.
- If the child process exits before readiness, the script can collapse that into a generic “did not report ready” failure instead of reporting that the watch process actually exited.
- When the watch guard fails, stdout/stderr artifacts are written, but the actionable tail is not surfaced inline with the failure.
- The duplicate dist-runtime warning was gated too broadly; unrelated failures could print the split-runtime warning even when no dist-runtime graph regression was detected.

## What changed

- Prefer bash for the timed watch shell when available, while retaining `/bin/sh` fallback.
- Quote the Node executable path explicitly and put its directory at the front of `PATH` for the isolated watch process.
- Track child process exit during PID-file wait, readiness wait, settle, idle window, and stop phases.
- Report `gateway:watch exited before ready (...)` when the child exits before readiness.
- Include bounded stdout/stderr tails on watch failures.
- Only print the duplicate dist-runtime warning when a dist-runtime failure is actually present.
- Add focused unit coverage for:
  - bash selection/fallback
  - generated timed watch command/env
  - early exit before readiness
  - duplicate dist-runtime warning gating
  - stderr/stdout artifact metadata in the watch result

## Evidence

### Rebase/squash proof — 2026-06-23 UTC

Latest pushed commit after same-base rebase and squash: `4ea42a587047d35cb4f54814488f0ded22524eb7`.

All six coordinated PR branches in this batch were rebased onto the same pinned upstream base commit:

```text
dc9c11be917ebdc711b956250aa80a8e5b47bea6
```

Post-rebase local verification included conflict-marker/diff hygiene plus focused branch-specific gates. Final proof marker for this PR:

```text

commands.review_summary:
The PR broadens Feishu merge_forward sub-message filtering, formats interactive sub-messages through the existing card parser, and adds Feishu bot regression tests.

PR surface: Source +12, Tests +122. Total +134 across 3 files.

Reproducibility: yes. source-level: current main fetches merge_forward details into parseMergeForwardContent, whose formatter lacks an interactive case and whose filter drops items without upper_message_id. I did not run a live Feishu tenant reproduction in this read-only review.
commands.review_followup: - [P1] The branch needs contributor or maintainer revision plus real behavior proof; automation should not bypass the external proof gate.

target.title: fix(feishu): parse all merge-forward sub-messages and extract interactive card text
target.body:
## Summary

**Problem**: Feishu merge_forward messages containing interactive/card sub-messages (tables, dashboards, report cards) were silently dropped or rendered as bare `[interactive]`. Two bugs: (1) `parseMergeForwardContent` only included sub-messages with `upper_message_id`, but interactive cards may lack this field; (2) `formatSubMessageContent` had no case for `"interactive"` and fell through to the generic `[contentType]` format.

**Solution**: (1) Broaden the sub-message filter to include all items except the root merge_forward container; (2) Add an `"interactive"` case that reuses `parseInteractiveCardContent` from `send.ts` — the same parser already used in production for fetched message card rendering.

**What changed**: 
- `extensions/feishu/src/bot-content.ts`: +14/-2 — filter fix + interactive card parsing via shared `parseInteractiveCardContent`
- `extensions/feishu/src/bot.test.ts`: +122 — 2 new regression tests (unit + integration with mocked Feishu API)
- `extensions/feishu/src/send.ts`: +1/-1 — export `parseInteractiveCardContent`

**What did NOT change**:
- The `parseInteractiveCardContent` parser itself — unchanged, just exported
- Other sub-message type handling (text, image, sticker, etc.) — unchanged
- The Feishu API fetch path for merge_forward detail — unchanged

Fixes #77909

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

**Behavior addressed**: Feishu merge_forward messages containing interactive/card sub-messages were dropped or rendered as bare `[interactive]` placeholder text. Card header titles, table data, and body content were lost before reaching the agent.

**Real setup tested**:
- **Runtime**: Node 24.13, Linux x86_64
- **Branch**: `fix/issue-77909-feishu-merge-forward` rebased onto `upstream/main` (`8c09419f2`)
- **Test runner**: `scripts/run-vitest.mjs` (project-standard wrapper)
- **Vitest**: 4.1.8

**Root cause — two independent bugs in the same code path**:

Bug 1 (filter): `parseMergeForwardContent` at `bot-content.ts:230` originally used:
```typescript
const subMessages = items.filter((item) => item.upper_message_id);
```
Interactive/card items returned by the Feishu merge_forward detail API may not include `upper_message_id`. These items were filtered out before reaching `formatSubMessageContent`, so their content was completely lost.

Bug 2 (format): `formatSubMessageContent` in `bot-content.ts:198` had no case for `contentType === "interactive"`, falling through to `default: return `[${contentType}]``. Even if interactive items passed the filter, they'd render as `[interactive]` with no extracted text.

**Detailed fix trace** (specific code changes in `bot-content.ts`):

Filter fix (`bot-content.ts:235-238`):
```typescript
const subMessages = items.filter(
  (item) => Boolean(item.upper_message_id) || item.msg_type !== "merge_forward",
);
```
Now includes items with `upper_message_id` (original behavior) OR items that are NOT root merge_forward containers (new: catches interactive cards, images, text without parent ref).

Format fix (`bot-content.ts:201-203`):
```typescript
case "interactive":
  return parseInteractiveCardContent(parsed);
```
Reuses the production-grade parser that handles template variables (`{variable}`), i18n element tags (`{{i18n key}}`), body.elements (div, markdown, lark_md), and post content fallback. This is the same parser used by `getMessageFeishu` for regular fetched messages.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run extensions/feishu/src/bot.test.ts` on the rebased `fix/issue-77909-feishu-merge-forward` branch at Node 24.13 Linux x86_64

**After-fix evidence**:
```
[test] starting test/vitest/vitest.extension-feishu.config.ts
 RUN  v4.1.8
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  89 passed (89)
[test] passed 1 Vitest shard in 23.79s
```

Two new regression tests added:

1. **Unit test**: "includes interactive sub-messages without upper_message_id (regression #77909)" — directly calls `parseMergeForwardContent` with a card containing header "Weekly Report" and Sales/Cost body elements. Verifies card text is extracted when `upper_message_id` is absent.

2. **Integration test**: "expands merge_forward interactive card sub-messages (regression #77909)" — via `dispatchMessage` with mocked Feishu API returning an interactive card "Q2 Sales Dashboard" with Revenue/Profit data. Verifies `BodyForAgent` contains header title, body text, and accompanying text message.

**Observed result after the fix**: Card content like headers ("Q2 Sales Dashboard"), table data ("Revenue: ¥50,000", "Profit: ¥12,300"), and markdown body text now correctly reach the agent's `BodyForAgent`. The shared `parseInteractiveCardContent` parser ensures consistent rendering between merge_forward interactive cards and directly-fetched interactive messages. Before: `[interactive]` or silently dropped; after: full card text extraction via shared production parser.

**What was not tested**: Live Feishu tenant with real merge_forward messages containing interactive cards. This requires a Feishu app with bot credentials, a group chat with merge_forward messages containing interactive card content, and active WebSocket event delivery. Feishu/Lark tenant access is not available for external contributors — see note below.

**Evidence provenance**: Terminal output from real local checkout of rebased `fix/issue-77909-feishu-merge-forward` on Node 24.13.1 / Linux x86_64, 2026-06-23. The two prior commits (`ea066aeb8` and `74ecd7198`) were squashed into one clean commit, and the CI re-run trigger commit (`e047257a5`) was dropped.

## Tests and validation
| Test Type | Result | Details |
|-----------|--------|---------|
| Unit Tests | ✅ 89/89 | bot.test.ts — all passing including 2 new regression tests |
| Rebase | ✅ | Clean rebase onto upstream/main, 1 squashed commit |
| Scope | ✅ | 3 feishu files only, CI trigger commit removed |

## Design decisions

**Why reuse `parseInteractiveCardContent` from `send.ts` instead of a simpler local extractor?**

The first implementation (commit `ea066aeb8`, later superseded) used a local `extractInteractiveText()` that only handled basic header/body text traversal. ClawSweeper review correctly identified this as a maintenance risk — two parsers for the same card format would inevitably drift over time as the Feishu card schema evolves or new features are added to the production parser.

The shared parser in `send.ts` handles a comprehensive set of Feishu interactive card features: template variable substitution (`{variable}` → resolved value), i18n element tags (`{{i18n key}}` → translated text via the i18n registry), body.elements iteration (div with text content, markdown blocks, lark_md inline formatting), post content fallback (cards that embed their text in a post-style content field), and config-level text extraction. All of these are potentially relevant to merge_forward interactive cards, which can contain the same card types as directly-fetched messages.

Exporting the function (`function` → `export function`) is a minimal API surface change — the function signature, behavior, and callers are all unchanged. The only difference is that `bot-content.ts` can now import and call it for merge_forward cards. This is the same pattern used by `bot-content.ts`'s existing import of `saveMessageResourceFeishu` from `media.js` — cross-module imports within the same extension are standard practice.

**Why "include all non-root items" instead of "include items with known msg_types"?**

A whitelist of known types would silently drop any future Feishu message types added to merge_forward payloads. The exclusion-based approach ("everything except the root container") is forward-compatible: new message types automatically pass through to `formatSubMessageContent`, which handles unknown types gracefully via the `[${contentType}]` fallback that renders a readable placeholder rather than dropping content entirely.

The root container is excluded because it's always `msg_type: "merge_forward"` and contains only wrapper metadata (merge_forward header/title), not user-visible sub-message content. Including it would produce a duplicate/nested "Merged and Forwarded Message" label. The exclusion uses `item.msg_type !== "merge_forward"` as a negative check — any item whose msg_type IS merge_forward is the container, and any item whose msg_type is anything else (text, image, interactive, sticker, etc.) is a sub-message worth rendering.

**Why can't this be tested with a live Feishu tenant?**

Feishu open platform requires enterprise/organization certification for app creation. Lark international version is more permissive for individual developers but may require network access that is not available from all environments. The OpenClaw QA credential pool (Convex broker) requires write/maintain repository permissions that external contributors do not have. The fix is validated by unit tests using exact Feishu interactive card JSON fixtures matching the documented card message schema, including header.title with plain_text tags, body.elements with div/markdown/lark_md content, and the standard Feishu message envelope structure.

**Why can't this be tested with a live Feishu tenant?**

Feishu open platform requires enterprise/org certification for app creation (personal developers may face restrictions). Lark international version is more permissive but requires network access that may not be available. The OpenClaw QA credential pool (Convex broker) requires write/maintain repository permissions. External contributors cannot access any of these paths. The fix is validated by unit tests using exact Feishu card JSON fixtures matching the documented interactive card schema.

## Risk checklist

**What is the highest-risk area of this change?**

The broadened sub-message filter could theoretically include unexpected item types from the Feishu API that were previously excluded. If the Feishu API returns items with `msg_type` values not handled by `formatSubMessageContent`, they would render as `[unknown_type]` instead of being silently dropped.

**Risk level**: Low risk — `formatSubMessageContent` has a `default: return `[${contentType}]`` fallback for unknown types, so any unexpected sub-message types from the Feishu API produce visible-but-safe placeholder text rather than crashing the handler or leaking raw JSON data into the agent body. The root container exclusion relies on `msg_type !== "merge_forward"` which is the stable API contract for merge_forward wrapper items — this field has been consistent across Feishu API versions.

**Mitigation**: The two new regression tests cover both the filter path (items without `upper_message_id`) and the format path (interactive card content extraction). The integration test mocks the full Feishu API round-trip: `im.message.get` returning merge_forward detail items, `dispatchMessage` processing through the complete handler chain, and `finalizeInboundContext` producing the final agent body. The unit test directly exercises `parseMergeForwardContent` with the exact card JSON fixture from the issue report. The existing 87 bot tests verify zero regressions in other Feishu message handling paths. The `parseInteractiveCardContent` parser is production-proven — it's the same code path used for every directly-fetched Feishu interactive message.

**Merge risk declaration**:
- `merge-risk: 🚨 message-delivery` — Changes what the agent sees for merge_forward messages. Previously dropped or placeholder content is now rendered as readable text. This is a bug fix restoring correct behavior: the agent should see card content, not `[interactive]`.

## Current review state
**What is the next action?** Maintainer review requested. Code changes are mature (one review cycle completed), all 89 feishu tests pass, and the `triage: needs-real-behavior-proof` gate requires maintainer decision on proof adequacy.

target.title: Doctor: expose session artifact findings
target.body:
## Summary

- Exposes legacy session transcript state as structured Doctor findings.
- Exposes stale cached session snapshot paths as structured Doctor findings.
- Treats session artifact findings as advisory `info` findings so default `doctor --lint` does not fail existing users with legacy artifacts.
- Adds dry-run repair effects for transcript rewrites and snapshot path rewrites.
- Keeps the existing legacy Doctor `run` paths authoritative for real `doctor` / `doctor --fix` mutations.
- Does not add or change config, plugin, or public SDK contract surface.

This is intentionally the next narrow session-artifacts slice before returning to the broader dry-run preview PR. It does not change transcript repair behavior, snapshot repair behavior, session store target resolution, or the public health-check contract.

## Stack

Rebased onto current `main` at `7668a72843d8437e193e0c3ed2467f88a8741512`. Current branch head is `c77bedfb7d` (`fix(doctor): make session artifact findings advisory`).

## Validation

Current head `c77bedfb7d`:

- `OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/commands/doctor-session-transcripts.test.ts --reporter=dot` - 9 tests passed

target.title: feat(cron): include event time in failure alerts
target.body:
## Summary

Closes #77497.

Cron failure notifications now include the failed event time instead of relying only on the delivery timestamp. The timestamp uses the cron schedule timezone when present and falls back to UTC for non-cron schedules or missing timezones.

## What changed

- Added a shared cron alert timestamp formatter.
- Included event time in single failure destination notifications.
- Included the last event time in repeated failure and skipped-run alerts.
- Included event time in schedule-error auto-disable notifications.
- Plumbed the event timestamp through failure alert delivery params and webhook payloads.

## User impact

Delayed cron alerts are less misleading after gateway restarts or host connectivity outages because the alert body names when the cron failure happened.

## Validation

- `CI=true OPENCLAW_LOCAL_CHECK=0 npm exec pnpm@10.9.0 -- test src/cron/service.failure-alert.test.ts src/gateway/server.cron.test.ts src/cron/service/jobs.schedule-error-isolation.test.ts`
- `CI=true OPENCLAW_LOCAL_CHECK=0 npm exec pnpm@10.9.0 -- exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/cron/alert-time.ts src/gateway/server-cron-notifications.ts src/cron/service/timer.ts src/cron/service/state.ts src/gateway/server-cron.ts src/cron/service/jobs.ts src/cron/service.failure-alert.test.ts src/gateway/server.cron.test.ts src/cron/service/jobs.schedule-error-isolation.test.ts`
- `CI=true OPENCLAW_LOCAL_CHECK=0 npm exec pnpm@10.9.0 -- exec tsgo -b tsconfig.projects.json`
- `CI=true OPENCLAW_LOCAL_CHECK=0 npm exec pnpm@10.9.0 -- run check:changed`

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Cron failure alert text now includes the cron event timestamp so delayed channel delivery does not look like a fresh failure.
- **Real environment tested**: Local OpenClaw checkout in `/private/tmp/openclaw-cron-alert-timestamps` on macOS, Node v25.8.1, pnpm 10.33.2.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: Ran the cron alert formatter directly from the local checkout and ran the focused cron/gateway validation commands listed above.
- **Evidence after fix**: Terminal output from the patched formatter:

```text
$ node --import tsx -e "import { formatCronAlertEventTime } from './src/cron/alert-time.ts'; console.log(formatCronAlertEventTime({ job: { schedule: { kind: 'cron', expr: '0 11 * * *', tz: 'Asia/Dubai' } }, eventTimeMs: Date.parse('2026-05-04T07:20:00.000Z') })); console.log(formatCronAlertEventTime({ job: { schedule: { kind: 'every', everyMs: 60000 } }, eventTimeMs: Date.parse('2026-05-04T07:20:00.000Z') }));"
2026-05-04 11:20 (Asia/Dubai)
2026-05-04 07:20 (UTC)
```

- **Observed result after fix**: The formatter emits a human-readable event time in the scheduled timezone and falls back to UTC when no cron timezone exists; the focused gateway/cron tests verify those timestamps appear in failure alert bodies.
- **What was not tested**: I did not run a live Telegram outage/reconnect scenario; validation covered local gateway notification paths and cron service alert generation.

target.title: [codex] Further optimize Telegram media and reply context flow
target.body:
## Summary

- add scoped indexes to the Telegram message cache so `recentBefore` and `around` avoid scanning and sorting the full cache
- cache successful Telegram media resolutions by file identity and relevant runtime options
- revalidate cached media paths before reuse so TTL cleanup cannot leave stale attachments behind
- add regression coverage for thread-scoped chat windows, media cache hits, and stale media cache eviction
- bump `@openclaw/fs-safe` to 0.2.1 so focused Telegram tests can install and resolve `@openclaw/fs-safe/config` cleanly

## Context

This builds on earlier Telegram-route performance work by `obviyus`, further optimizing the same flow by tightening reply-context lookups and avoiding repeated Telegram media hydration.

## Why

Telegram reply-context building can repeatedly query recent chat windows and hydrate the same reply media. This PR keeps those hot paths bounded while preserving the existing chat-wide and thread-specific behavior.

The media cache also checks the saved file still exists before returning a cached path, because long-running gateways can remove saved media through TTL cleanup.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Telegram reply-context lookups stay thread-scoped or chat-wide as requested, repeated Telegram media hydration reuses the cached saved path, and a deleted saved media file is evicted and re-downloaded instead of returning a stale path.
- **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw gateway from this PR head (`b8ad83191132ea2cdb1c9a40312ded58f58444ba`) on macOS, running `OpenClaw 2026.5.10-beta.1` with the Telegram plugin loaded from the PR checkout. Telegram ingress used the real Bot API webhook for `@acecoderbot` through a temporary Cloudflare tunnel. The LLM provider was mocked only to make the response deterministic; Telegram update delivery, reply-context hydration, message-cache persistence, and reply dispatch all ran through the PR checkout.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran `pnpm openclaw --log-level debug gateway run --port 19043 --auth token --token [redacted] --bind loopback --verbose --ws-log compact` from the PR checkout with an isolated `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR`, a real Telegram Bot API webhook, and a deterministic local OpenAI-compatible provider. Sent `PR80243 live seed OPENCLAW_E2E_OK_1` to `@acecoderbot`, then sent a real Telegram reply to that DM with `PR80243 live reply-context OPENCLAW_E2E_OK_2`. Also ran a local media/cache proof through `node --import tsx` using a real temporary state directory and local trusted Bot API file path.
- **Evidence after fix:** Redacted live runtime logs and persisted cache/model-input excerpts from the actual PR gateway run:

```text

The supplemental cache/media proof showed thread-scoped windows stayed scoped, chat-wide windows included cross-topic history, the second media resolution reused the cached path without another `getFile`, and stale saved-path eviction forced a fresh `getFile`.

target.title: fix(acp): reject fractional runtime timeouts
target.body:
## Summary
- reject fractional numeric ACP runtime timeout patches instead of silently rounding them
- add regression coverage for fractional `timeoutSeconds` values

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** ACP runtime option validation should reject fractional numeric `timeoutSeconds` values instead of silently rounding them. Integer seconds should still be accepted.

**Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout on macOS after `pnpm install`, branch `fix/acp-runtime-timeout-integer`, commit `571ad9f7d6`.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran a live Node command with `tsx` import support against the local checkout; called `validateRuntimeOptionPatch` with `1.4`, `1.6`, and integer `2`.

**Evidence after fix:** Terminal output from the live command:

```console
$ node --import tsx -e "import { validateRuntimeOptionPatch } from './src/acp/control-plane/runtime-options.ts'; for (const value of [1.4, 1.6]) { try { console.log(value, validateRuntimeOptionPatch({ timeoutSeconds: value })); } catch (error) { console.log(value, error instanceof Error ? error.message : String(error)); } } console.log('integer', JSON.stringify(validateRuntimeOptionPatch({ timeoutSeconds: 2 })));"
1.4 Timeout must be a positive integer in seconds.
1.6 Timeout must be a positive integer in seconds.
integer {"timeoutSeconds":2}
```

**Observed result after fix:** Fractional numeric timeout patches now fail with `Timeout must be a positive integer in seconds.`, while an integer timeout patch still returns `{ "timeoutSeconds": 2 }`.

**What was not tested:** Full live ACP client session. The proof exercises the actual runtime-options module in a real local OpenClaw checkout; unit and changed-scope checks cover the integration surface.

## Test Plan
- `pnpm test src/acp/control-plane/runtime-options.test.ts src/acp/control-plane/manager.test.ts`
- `pnpm check:changed`

## Notes
The string/config-option path already requires integer seconds via `parseRuntimeTimeoutSecondsInput`; this aligns the numeric runtime patch path with that contract.

target.title: fix(gateway): archive legacy-root stale transcript files on reset/delete
target.body:
Fixes #91868.

## Summary

When a stale legacy transcript path such as `~/.openclaw/sessions/<old-uuid>.jsonl` is stored in session state, `resolveSessionFilePath()` silently normalizes it to a generated path for the *current* sessionId. The legacy file therefore never appears in `resolveSessionTranscriptCandidates()` and is never archived by `/clear` or `/reset`, so it keeps getting re-attached to later prompts.

This PR adds a small containment-checked helper (`pushRawStaleLegacyCandidate`) that appends the raw stale path as an archive candidate only when it resolves under the OpenClaw-owned legacy transcript root (`~/.openclaw/sessions/`), with `fs.realpathSync`-based canonicalization to prevent symlink escape. It also adds archive-flow regression tests proving the legacy file is actually renamed on both `reset` and `deleted`, while outside-root and symlink-escape paths stay untouched.

Thanks to @Pandah97 in #92751 for identifying the same fix location; this change provides the archive-flow proof requested by ClawSweeper.

## Verification

```bash
pnpm test src/gateway/session-utils.fs.test.ts --run
pnpm test src/gateway/session-transcript-files.fs.archive-events.test.ts --run
```

- `session-utils.fs.test.ts`: 364 tests pass (includes new archive-flow regression tests).
- `session-transcript-files.fs.archive-events.test.ts`: 20 tests pass (no regressions).

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed:** After `/clear` or `/reset`, stale legacy `~/.openclaw/sessions/<old-uuid>.jsonl` transcripts persist and are re-attached.

**Real environment tested:** Local Linux checkout, Node v22.22.0, pnpm 11.2.2.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

```bash
cd /home/0668000780/开源社区提交2/openclaw
node /tmp/repro-91868.mjs
```

**Evidence after fix:**

```
=== Scenario 1: legacy-root stale file (should be archived) ===
archived: ['/tmp/.../.openclaw/sessions/22222222-2222-1222-8222-222222222222.jsonl.reset.2026-06-15T06-12-04.656Z']
original exists: false
archive exists: true

=== Scenario 2: outside-root stale file (should NOT be archived) ===
archived: []
original exists: true

=== Scenario 3: symlink under legacy root pointing outside (should NOT be archived) ===
archived: []
target exists: true
```

**Observed result after fix:** Legacy-root stale `.jsonl` files are renamed to `.jsonl.reset.<ts>` / `.jsonl.deleted.<ts>`; outside-root and symlink-escape files are left untouched.

**What was not tested:** Production `/clear` or `/reset` live command dispatch; behavior is covered by the archive-flow regression tests added in `session-utils.fs.test.ts`.

target.title: Refresh MCP OAuth auth-profile tokens
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Remote MCP servers such as Ducktape can be configured with static `Authorization` headers today. That makes an otherwise refresh-capable OAuth credential decay into dead bearer text, so tool discovery/calls fail once the access token expires.

This PR lets OpenClaw own that refresh path for remote MCP servers by binding a server to an existing refresh-capable auth profile:

```json
{
  "mcp": {
    "servers": {
      "ducktape": {
        "url": "https://agents.ducktape.xyz/mcp",
        "transport": "streamable-http",
        "auth": "oauth",
        "oauth": {
          "authProfileId": "ducktape:mcp"
        }
      }
    }
  }
}
```

## Summary

- Added `mcp.servers.*.oauth.authProfileId` to config schema/types/help/labels/docs.
- Added an MCP auth-profile resolver that:
  - loads the selected auth profile,
  - requires it to be a refreshable OAuth profile,
  - reuses `resolveApiKeyForProfile` for refresh/locking/store semantics,
  - strips stale static `Authorization` headers,
  - never projects refresh tokens downstream.
- Wired auth-profile bearer injection into:
  - embedded/session MCP HTTP transports,
  - CLI bundle MCP projection via per-server token env vars,
  - native Codex app-server user MCP projection.
- Redacted literal app-server Authorization headers before computing the user-MCP resume fingerprint so access tokens do not become sticky session binding text.

## Related Issues / Context

Fixes #91585. James linked the Ducktape MCP expired-token failure and asked OpenClaw to own token refresh instead of relying on a James-specific cron or static config rewrite.

This keeps existing MCP-native OAuth login behavior unchanged: `auth: "oauth"` without `oauth.authProfileId` still uses the MCP SDK OAuth provider and `openclaw mcp login`.

## Example / Behavior Sketch

- `auth: "oauth"` + no `oauth.authProfileId`:
  - existing MCP OAuth flow remains active;
  - static `Authorization` headers are still ignored on OAuth servers.
- `auth: "oauth"` + `oauth.authProfileId`:
  - OpenClaw resolves the auth profile before runtime projection;
  - static/stale `Authorization` is removed;
  - downstream MCP config gets only the current bearer access token/header;
  - static `token` profiles fail clearly instead of being treated as refreshable.

## Evidence

Focused tests:

```text
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/mcp-auth-profile.test.ts src/agents/mcp-transport.test.ts src/agents/cli-runner/bundle-mcp.codex.test.ts src/agents/cli-runner/bundle-mcp.user-config.test.ts src/agents/cli-runner/bundle-mcp-codex.user-config.test.ts src/config/schema.test.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/thread-lifecycle.user-mcp-servers.test.ts
```

Result: 4 Vitest shards passed, covering 98 tests.

Lint / type / whitespace:

```text
node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs src/agents/mcp-auth-profile.ts src/agents/mcp-auth-profile.test.ts src/agents/mcp-transport.ts src/agents/agent-bundle-mcp-runtime.ts src/agents/cli-runner/bundle-mcp.ts src/agents/cli-runner/prepare.ts src/agents/cli-runner/bundle-mcp-codex.ts src/agents/cli-runner/bundle-mcp-codex.user-config.test.ts src/plugin-sdk/codex-mcp-projection.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/thread-lifecycle.ts src/config/types.mcp.ts src/config/zod-schema.ts src/config/schema.test.ts src/config/schema.labels.ts src/config/schema.help.ts
```

Result: passed.

```text
pnpm tsgo:test:src
```

Result: passed.

```text
git diff --check
```

Result: passed.

## Remaining Limitations

- This PR adds the generic OpenClaw-owned refresh/projection path, but it does not mutate any live operator config such as Hedwig's current Ducktape server entry.
- Live Ducktape proof still requires a real `ducktape:mcp` style OAuth auth profile binding in config. No real credentials are included in this branch or PR.

The branch changes memory-wiki lint to accept bridge-relative source-page links, ignore reply directive wikilinks, and add a regression test.

PR surface: Source +42, Tests +65. Total +107 across 2 files.

Reproducibility: yes. from source inspection rather than live execution: current main normalizes Markdown links against the generated wiki page path and broken-link lint validates only path/basename targets, so bridge-origin relative links can be misresolved. I did not run a live lint command because this review is read-only and lint writes reports/logs.
